headline: Fishers Become Ocean Guardians in Cape Verde
meta_description: In Cape Verde, 190 fishers volunteer to monitor illegal fishing and protect marine life, turning daily work into conservation.
summary_short: A 10-year-old program in Cape Verde trains fishers as ocean observers, combining livelihoods with marine protection.
body:
When Alberto Martius brings his wooden boat back to shore on the island of Fogo, he carries more than the day's catch. The Cabo Verde fisher and his 18-year-old son Leandro are part of the Guardians of the Sea program, a volunteer initiative that asks fishers to monitor illegal activities and marine megafauna while doing what they do best.

"I'm proud my son is following in my footsteps, protecting the ocean," Martius told Mongabay. "Almost every day, I'm patrolling this area to monitor marine animals."

Launched in 2016 on the island of Maio, the program has grown to include 190 fishers across seven of the archipelago's ten islands. The initiative taps into a reality that authorities cannot match: fishers are already at sea, already know the waters, and already command respect among their peers.

On Maio alone, guardians completed 139,181 patrol trips and recorded 513 instances of illegal activity between 2016 and 2025. They've spotted humpback whales, lemon sharks, and loggerhead turtles—data that helps map marine patterns and inform conservation.

The presence of respected guardians deters illegal fishing and raises awareness about sustainable practices, says Sara Ratão, who helped develop the program. With limited government enforcement capacity, these community-led efforts have become essential to protecting Cabo Verde's fishing heritage for future generations.
